content.text: Why GenAI Discovery Interviews Differ

Standard user interviews surface what people do and what frustrates them. GenAI discovery interviews must additionally surface three things that standard interviews do not: the user's mental model of what AI can and cannot do, the constraints on AI autonomy they will accept, and their quality bar for AI-generated outputs. Without these three signals, the requirements team will build to their own assumptions about appropriate AI scope — which almost always differs from the users who must work with the system every day.

A second structural difference: the most valuable insight often comes from observing how users react to an actual AI output, not to a wireframe or description. Showing a real model response for a domain-specific task in Phase 4 of the interview surfaces edge cases, quality bar expectations, and trust thresholds that hypothetical questions never reach.

attrs.alt: GenAI user interview structure: four phases with timing and goals

content.text: Interview Guide: FNOL Claims Chatbot Discovery

The guide below follows the four-phase structure. Phases 1 and 2 run before any AI is mentioned — this prevents framing bias where users evaluate their work through an AI lens rather than describing what they actually experience.

# GenAI feature discovery interview guide (45-minute format)
# Role: claims handler | Feature area: FNOL chatbot

INTRO = """
Thank you for your time. I'm researching how claims handlers currently work,
to understand what genuinely helps versus what gets in the way. I am not
evaluating your performance — there are no right or wrong answers.
I may take notes. Do you consent to this session being recorded? [confirm]
"""

# ── Phase 1: Context (10-15 min) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
CONTEXT_QUESTIONS = [
    # Anchor to concrete recent experience — never ask "in general"
    "Can you walk me through the last FNOL claim you handled from the moment it came in?",
    "What information did you need to gather first?",
    "Which systems or tools did you use, and in what order?",
    "Roughly how long did that claim take from receipt to being ready for assessment?",
    # Comparative follow-up
    "Is that typical? What makes some claims take much longer?",
]

# ── Phase 2: Pain points (10-15 min) ─────────────────────────────────────────
PAIN_QUESTIONS = [
    "Which step in that process do you find most tedious or error-prone?",
    "Are there things you have to look up or check every time? What are they?",
    "Have you invented any workarounds or shortcuts that aren't in the official process?",
    "When does a claim get stuck waiting — for you, or for someone else?",
    "What information do claimants give you that is confusing or incomplete?",
    # Critical incident technique
    "Can you tell me about a claim that went wrong recently — what happened?",
    "What would the ideal version of that have looked like?",
]

# ── Phase 3: Mental model (10 min) ───────────────────────────────────────────
MENTAL_MODEL_QUESTIONS = [
    # Introduce AI carefully — after building context
    "Some teams use AI assistants to help with tasks like this. Have you used any?",
    "What did it do well? What did it get wrong?",
    # Hypothetical framing (not leading)
    "If you had an assistant that could read claim submissions and draft a summary, "
    "what would you need to check before you'd trust that summary?",
    "Are there parts of your job where you would never want an AI to make a decision alone?",
    "How would you feel if the AI got something wrong that you hadn't caught?",
]

# ── Phase 4: Output probe (optional, 10 min) ─────────────────────────────────
# Show a real model output for a sample (anonymised) FNOL claim
OUTPUT_PROBE = """
[Show actual LLM output for an FNOL claim summary]

"Here is what an AI assistant produced for a claim similar to ones you handle.
Can you read through it and tell me your reaction as you go?
Point to anything that's wrong, missing, or that you'd rewrite."
"""

PROBE_FOLLOW_UPS = [
    "What specifically would you change in that summary?",
    "If this arrived in your queue and you had to act on it, what would you verify first?",
    "Is there anything here the AI confidently stated that you'd want a source for?",
    "Under what circumstances would you override the AI's categorisation completely?",
]

CLOSE = """
Thank you — this has been very helpful. Before we finish:
- Is there anything about your day-to-day that you wished I had asked about?
- Is there a colleague whose perspective would be different from yours and worth talking to?
"""
Question Design Principles

The most common interview mistake in GenAI discovery is asking solution-first questions: 'Would you use an AI for this?' or 'What features would you want?' These questions produce answers shaped by the user's imagination of AI, not their experience of their work. They also give the requirements team false confidence in feature ideas that have not been grounded in real need.

# Question anti-patterns in GenAI discovery interviews

# ✗ Leading question — implies AI is the answer
BAD  = "How would an AI assistant make this step easier?"
GOOD = "What would the ideal version of this step look like?"

# ✗ Hypothetical without grounding
BAD  = "Would you use an AI chatbot for FNOL if we built one?"
GOOD = "Walk me through what you'd do if you received this claim. [show example]"

# ✗ Yes/no question — no signal
BAD  = "Do you find the current system frustrating?"
GOOD = "Tell me about a recent time the system slowed you down."

# ✗ Solution-first framing
BAD  = "What features would you want in an AI summarisation tool?"
GOOD = "When you receive a new claim, what information do you need first?"

# ✗ Asking about AI before understanding current state
BAD  = "What part of your job do you think AI could help with?"
GOOD = (Phase 1 and 2 first, then) "If you had a smart assistant for that task..."

# ✗ Abstract / general
BAD  = "How do you generally handle FNOL claims?"
GOOD = "Walk me through the last FNOL claim you worked on."
Interview goal
Avoid
Use instead
Understand current workflow
General descriptions ('usually I…')
Critical incident: 'Tell me about the last time you…'
Identify pain points
Rating scales ('on a scale of 1–10…')
Behavioural evidence: 'What workarounds have you invented?'
Calibrate AI trust
Direct trust questions ('Do you trust AI?')
Hypothetical with stakes: 'If the AI got the category wrong, what would happen?'
Surface constraints
Permission questions ('Would it be OK if AI…?')
Boundary questions: 'What would you never want AI to decide alone?'
Validate quality bar
Rating AI on a scale
Reaction to real output: 'Point to what's wrong in this summary'
Participant Selection

GenAI discovery interviews require deliberate participant diversity on two axes: role (to capture different usage patterns and oversight responsibilities) and attitude toward AI (to capture both enthusiast feature requests and sceptic trust requirements). Recruiting only from willing participants over-samples AI enthusiasm and misses the constraint signals that sceptics provide.

# Participant selection matrix for FNOL chatbot discovery

PARTICIPANT_MATRIX = {
    # Role diversity
    "claims_handler_junior":  {"n": 3, "rationale": "Daily users; highest exposure to AI errors"},
    "claims_handler_senior":  {"n": 2, "rationale": "Expertise + quality bar calibration"},
    "team_leader":            {"n": 2, "rationale": "Oversight perspective; escalation patterns"},
    "claimant_facing_agent":  {"n": 1, "rationale": "Represents end-user trust perspective"},

    # Context diversity
    "high_volume_team":       {"n": 2, "rationale": "Different automation incentives from low-volume"},
    "specialist_claims":      {"n": 1, "rationale": "Edge cases; complexity ceiling for AI"},

    # Attitude diversity (recruit deliberately)
    "ai_enthusiast":          {"n": 2, "rationale": "Surfaces feature requests; test against constraint gaps"},
    "ai_sceptic":             {"n": 2, "rationale": "Surfaces trust requirements and failure modes"},
}

TOTAL = 8   # 6-8 is sufficient for most GenAI discovery rounds; diminishing returns after 10

Six to eight interviews typically reach saturation for a focused GenAI feature: the same pain points and constraints recur across participants after interview 5 or 6. Run a further 2–3 interviews only if a new participant profile (specialist role, different team) consistently surfaces novel signals.
Synthesis: From Raw Findings to Feature Constraints

The synthesis step separates researcher inference (AI opportunity) from user statement (pain point). The requirements team can challenge the inference; they cannot challenge the verbatim quote. Keep the two columns distinct in every synthesis artefact.
Mapping Findings to Requirements Artefacts
Interview phase
Primary output
Requirements artefact it feeds
Phase 1: Context
Current-state workflow map with timing
User journey; process scope definition
Phase 2: Pain points
Ranked pain point list with frequency and severity
Use case priority; ROI estimate inputs
Phase 3: Mental model
AI constraint list; trust threshold description
Guardrail requirements; escalation triggers; human-in-the-loop scope
Phase 4: Output probe
Specific output errors noted; quality bar statements
Accuracy NFRs; evaluation rubric; few-shot example selection
All phases
Verbatim constraint quotes
Acceptance criteria for edge cases
The Output Probe in Detail

The output probe (Phase 4) is the highest-value element of a GenAI discovery interview and the most often skipped. Showing a real AI output for the user's domain produces qualitatively different data from any description or prototype: users point to specific words, correct specific errors, and articulate exactly where they would not trust the system. This data directly drives the quality bar for the feature's evaluation harness.
Select a real (anonymised) example from the user's domain — not a fabricated sample.
Run the example through the actual model or a close proxy. Do not use a curated best-case output.
Show the output without commentary. Ask the user to react aloud as they read.
After they finish: 'What would you change?' → drives evaluation rubric.
Follow up: 'Under what circumstances would you override this entirely?' → drives escalation requirements.
Probe trust: 'If this was wrong and you hadn't caught it, what would the consequence be?' → drives severity classification.

The output probe consistently surfaces quality bar expectations that questionnaire-based requirements elicitation misses. Users who say 'I'd want it to be accurate' in the abstract say '≥ 90% of the time it should match what I'd write' when shown a real output — a threefold difference in precision.
Common Discovery Interview Mistakes for GenAI
Mistake
Consequence
Correction
Mentioning AI in Phase 1
User frames current workflow through AI lens; misses real pain points
Describe it as a workflow research interview; only introduce AI in Phase 3
Showing a polished / curated AI output
User sees best-case behaviour; underestimates failure modes
Use a real output from the actual model on their domain
Recruiting only AI enthusiasts
Feature wishlist without trust constraints; over-scoped AI autonomy
Deliberately recruit sceptics; they provide the constraint signal
Asking hypothetical preference questions
Users describe imagined preferences, not real behaviour
Anchor every question to a recent specific event
Skipping Phase 4
Quality bar remains abstract ('it should be accurate')
Always show a real output; it is the highest-signal 10 minutes
Not separating researcher inference from user statement
Requirements team conflates what user said with what researcher inferred
In synthesis: always show verbatim quote alongside AI opportunity column
Analysing Trust Thresholds from Interview Data

Trust threshold data from Phase 3 is the most actionable signal for GenAI feature design. It directly drives the human-in-the-loop requirements: which decisions the AI can make autonomously, which require a human review step, and which are entirely outside the AI's permitted scope.
Trust threshold level
User signal from interview
Feature design implication
Full automation accepted
'I'd let it do that without checking — it's mechanical'
AI acts autonomously; output written directly to system
Automation with spot-check
'I'd trust it 9 times out of 10, but I'd want to review occasionally'
AI acts; human review on sampled 10%; alert on low-confidence outputs
AI drafts, human approves
'I'd want to see what it suggests before it goes anywhere'
AI generates draft; human reviews and submits; UI shows diff from template
AI assists, human decides
'Give me the relevant facts and I'll make the call'
AI surfaces information; decision UI requires explicit human selection
Human only
'I would never let AI near that — if it goes wrong it's a complaint'
AI excluded from scope; requirements explicitly state human-only

Map each workflow step to a trust threshold level during synthesis. Any step where more than 30% of participants assign a different trust level requires further investigation — the disagreement usually reflects role differences (senior vs junior) or context differences (routine vs complex claims) that the feature design must accommodate.
Worked Example: Synthesising Three Interviews into Requirements
The following shows how raw interview signals from three participants translate into specific feature requirements for the FNOL chatbot.
Source
Raw interview signal
Requirement derived
P01 (Senior Handler) — Phase 2
'I retype the same submission fields from the email every time — takes 10 minutes'
FR-01: System pre-fills CRM fields from inbound submission text; handler reviews before saving
P01 (Senior Handler) — Phase 3
'Don't auto-submit anything — I need to review before it's saved'
NFR-TRUST-01: No AI action writes to CRM without explicit handler confirmation
P02 (Handler) — Phase 2
'When claimants are vague, I call them back — that call averages 20 minutes'
FR-02: AI chat captures structured damage description from claimant before human handoff
P02 (Handler) — Phase 3
'If the AI gets the damage category wrong, the whole claim goes to the wrong team'
NFR-ACCURACY-01: Damage category accuracy ≥ 96% on eval set; wrong category is blocking failure
P03 (Team Leader) — Phase 4 probe
'This summary says 'minor damage' but the claimant wrote 'total loss' — that's a critical error'
NFR-ACCURACY-02: Severity level must never be understated; false 'minor' classification is highest-severity failure
P03 (Team Leader) — Phase 3
'Urgency flags affect which claimant gets a callback first — I'd want a human on that'
NFR-TRUST-02: AI may surface urgency indicators but may not set urgency flag without team leader review

Notice the pattern: Phase 2 produces functional requirements (what the AI should do); Phase 3 produces trust and guardrail NFRs (what the AI must not do autonomously); Phase 4 produces accuracy NFRs (what constitutes failure). Each phase contributes a different requirement type.
Running Remote vs In-Person Interviews
Consideration
In-person
Remote (video call)
Workflow observation
Can observe screen and physical environment
Screen share only; miss physical context
Output probe
Can hand printout; observe facial reaction
Share screen; verbal reaction only; note eye movements harder
Rapport
Easier to build; more candid responses on sensitive topics
Adequate if camera is on; formal feeling can suppress candour
Note-taking
Second researcher takes notes in room
Second researcher can observe silently; use transcript if consented
Participant effort
Higher (travel or hosting); lower recruitment yield
Lower barrier; broader geographic reach for multi-site programmes
Preferred for GenAI discovery
Phase 4 output probe ideally in person
Phases 1–3 work well remotely; Phase 4 acceptable with good screen share

For enterprise GenAI programmes spanning multiple office locations, run Phases 1–3 remotely and reserve in-person sessions for the output probe where observing reaction to the AI output is most valuable. A 45-minute remote interview followed by a 30-minute in-person output session is a practical hybrid.
Handling Resistance and Sensitive Topics

GenAI discovery interviews frequently encounter resistance that is absent in standard user research: concerns about job displacement, fear that reported inefficiencies will trigger headcount reductions, and distrust of corporate AI initiatives. These concerns suppress honest responses if not addressed explicitly at the start of the session.
Resistance type
Signal in session
How to address it
Job displacement anxiety
Minimal answers; deflects to 'the system works fine'
Explicitly state: 'We are researching how to remove tedious work — not reduce headcount. The goal is to give you time for higher-value tasks.'
Performance evaluation fear
Describes ideal process, not actual process
Emphasise no-judgment framing at the start; say 'I'm studying the process, not your performance'
Distrust of AI initiative
Negative framing of all AI; resistance to Phase 3 questions
Acknowledge: 'Some AI tools have been poorly implemented. Part of what I'm here to learn is what would actually help vs what wouldn't.'
Confidentiality concerns
Reluctance to describe specific claim details
Use fictional or anonymised examples in the output probe; never require real claim details
Seniority deference
Junior participant gives answers they think management wants
Interview senior and junior staff separately; reassure that all views are equally valuable

Resistance data is itself a signal. A team where multiple participants deflect questions about pain points either has a genuinely smooth process (rare) or a culture of risk suppression (common in regulated industries). If resistance is widespread, escalate to the programme sponsor: the discovery process cannot function without candid participation.
Post-Interview Debrief and Documentation
Conduct a 15-minute debrief immediately after each interview, before memory fades. The debrief captures impressions, tensions, and signals that did not make it into notes during the session.

# Interview debrief template (completed within 30 minutes of session end)

DEBRIEF_TEMPLATE = """
Interview: {participant_id} | {role} | {date}
Interviewer: {name}

TOP 3 FINDINGS (most important signals from this session):
1.
2.
3.

QUOTES TO KEEP VERBATIM (exact words worth citing in requirements docs):
-
-

TRUST THRESHOLD ASSESSMENT:
Most constrained: [which step did they most resist AI handling?]
Most open to AI: [which step did they most readily accept AI for?]

QUALITY BAR SIGNALS (from output probe, if run):
- Errors they noticed:
- What they said they'd change:
- Severity of errors they identified:

SURPRISES (anything not anticipated by the research questions):

HYPOTHESES CONFIRMED / DISCONFIRMED:
Confirmed:
Disconfirmed:

SUGGESTED FOLLOW-UP (anyone to speak to, topic to explore further):
"""

After every 3 sessions, run a cross-interview synthesis: stack the debrief cards and identify patterns that recur across participants. A pain point mentioned by 5 of 6 participants is a high-confidence finding; one mentioned by 1 of 6 is exploratory and should not anchor a requirement without further validation.
Frequency and Cadence of Discovery Interviews

Discovery interviews are not a one-time activity. GenAI features undergo significant changes during development (prompt updates, model changes, scope changes) that shift the quality bar and trust thresholds. A three-wave cadence is standard for enterprise delivery:
Wave
Timing
Purpose
Participant count
Wave 1: Formative
Before development begins
Identify pain points, establish current-state baseline, set quality bar expectations
6–8 participants
Wave 2: Evaluative
After first working prototype (before launch)
Validate AI scope against Phase 3 trust thresholds; re-run output probe with real model
4–6 participants (can overlap with Wave 1)
Wave 3: Post-launch
6–8 weeks after production launch
Validate that actual usage matches discovered needs; surface new pain points
4–6 participants (include new users not in Wave 1)

Wave 2 is the most commonly skipped and the most consequential. It is the only opportunity to validate that the AI's actual behaviour matches the trust thresholds established in Wave 1. Skipping it means the first real validation happens in production — where correction is expensive and trust recovery is slow.
Summary

GenAI discovery interviews follow a four-phase structure: context (current workflow before any mention of AI), pain points (what fails and why), mental model (trust calibration and constraint surfacing), and optional output probe (reaction to a real AI output). The output probe is the most valuable and most skipped phase — it converts abstract quality expectations into measurable thresholds. Questions must anchor to specific recent events, not hypothetical preferences. Participant selection must include AI sceptics to capture constraint requirements that enthusiasts do not surface. Synthesis separates verbatim user statements from researcher inferences; only the former is uncontestable evidence for a requirement.
